sanskrit_devanagari:
नैते सृती पार्थ जानन्योगी मुह्यति कश्चन ।
तस्मात्सर्वेषु कालेषु योगयुक्तो भवार्जुन ॥ २७ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
naite sṛtī pārtha jānan yogī muhyati kaścana
tasmāt sarveṣu kāleṣu yogayukto bhavārjuna || 27 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (canonical body text; vedabase concatenates the two halves; danda restored from gitasupersite)
exact (text body identical; verse-number marker rendered as ।।8.27।।)
exact IAST (verbatim from page; sandhi conventions match GRETIL)

translations.verbatim_quote: Although the devotees know these two paths, O Arjuna, they are never bewildered. Therefore be always fixed in devotion.
O Parth, knowing these two paths, the yogi is not bewildered. Therefore, O Arjun, at all times be steadfast in yog (union with God).

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Kṛṣṇa is here advising Arjuna that he should not be disturbed by the different paths the soul can take when leaving the material world. A devotee of the Supreme Lord should not worry whether he will depart by arrangement or by accident.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Prabhupada glosses BG 8.27 in the Gaudiya Vaishnava key.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 8.27 O son of Prtha, na kascana yogi, no yogi whosoever; janan, has known; ete srti, these two courses as described-that one leads to worldly life, and the other to Liberation; muhyati, becomes deluded. Tasmat, therefore; O Arjuna, bhava, be you; yoga-yuktah, steadfast in Yoga; sarvesu kal…
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads BG 8.27 in the Advaita non-dual key.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 8.27 Having known these two paths, no Yogin is deluded. On the contrary, he goes by the path of gods, his own path. Therefore, be integrated every day with Yoga called meditation on the path described by the terms starting with light. Next Sri Krsna speaks of the fruit of knowing the impor…
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads BG 8.27 in the Vishishtadvaita devotional-qualified key.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: By explaining the result of knowledge regarding the path of light which leads to moksa or liberation and the path of darkness which leads to samsara or the perpetual cycle of birth and death, this subject is being concluded by Lord Krishna. The yogi or one perfecting the science of the ind…
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Sridhara provides a synthetic gloss bridging Advaita and devotional readings.

positions.rendering: be always fixed in devotion (Kṛṣṇa consciousness)
at all times be steadfast in yog (union with God)
no yogi who has known these two courses becomes deluded; therefore at all times be steadfast in Yoga
knowing the two paths, the yogi goes by the path of gods; therefore be integrated every day with Yoga as meditation on the bright path

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The first half (knowing the two paths, no yogi is deluded) is anchored in 8.23-8.26. The second half (sarveṣu kāleṣu yogayuktaḥ) generalises 8.14's nityasaḥ principle: continuous discipline rather than situational. Advaita reads yoga generally; Vishishtadvaita reads it as meditation on the bright path. Gaudiya reads it as Kṛṣṇa-consciousness. The second half has a thin operational shadow; the first half is anchored in eschatology the engineering layer cannot reach.

literal_meaning: Knowing these two paths, no yogi is deluded; therefore at all times, Arjuna, be yoked in yoga.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Knowing these two paths, no yogi is deluded; therefore at all times be yoked in yoga. The verse generalises 8.14's nityasaḥ principle (continuous discipline) and applies it given the eschatological knowledge of 8.23-8.26. Shankara: no yogi who knows the two courses becomes deluded. Ramanuja: knowing the two paths, the yogi takes the bright; therefore be integrated daily.

remaining_gaps: Madhva did not comment on BG 8.27 (per Sarvabhashya tradition).
Ramanuja verbatim — page renders commentary in JS; bhagavad-gita.us static-HTML excerpt captured but full Adidevananda edition would deepen the citation.
